rulings.Case Title: MAJUA & NOYON LLC, A CALIFORNIA LIMITED LIABILITY VS. MG2 LLC, A WYOMING LIMITED LIABILITY COMPANY
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Petition To Confirm Contractual Arbitration Award And Enter Judgment On It
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law and Motion calendar for Monday , February 3, 2025 Line 1. PETITIONER MAJUA & NOYON LLC., Notice Of Petition To Confirm Contractual Arbitration Award And Enter Judgment On It. Continued to February 5, 2025 to be heard with companion petition to vacate contractual arbitration award. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 302 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 302 Zoom ID 160 409 7690; Passcode 516287.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. (302/JMQ)

rulings.Case Title: ERICA YINGLING ET AL VS. AVALONBAY COMMUNITIES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion By Defendants For Leave To Aemand An Independent Psychiatric Medical Examination Of Plaintiff Erica Yingling; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; And Declaration Of Alexander Cheung, In Support Thereof
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law and Motion calendar for Monday , February 3, 2025 Line 9. DEFENDANTs AVALONBAY COMMUNITIES, INC., AVALON AT MISSION BAY II, and MVP I, LLC.'s Motion By Defendants For Leave To Amend An Independent Psychiatric Medical Examination Of Plaintiff Erica Yingling. Defendants AVALONBAY COMMUNITIES, INC. and AVALON AT MISSION BAY II, MVP I, LLC's Motion By Defendants For Leave To Amend An Independent Psychiatric Medical Examination Of Plaintiff Erica Yingling is DENIED. CCP 2032.020 provides that any party may obtain discovery by means of a physical or mental examination of a party, in any action in which the mental or physical condition of that party is in controversy in the action. "As used in Code of Civil Procedure section [2032.020], the word 'condition' means 'state of being.' (Webster's Collegiate Dict. (10th Ed.1993) p. 240.) Logically, the statute's reference to a party's mental 'state of being' could only refer to the party's current mental state rather than mental conditions which had been experienced in the past but were no longer in existence." (Doyle v. Superior Court (1996) 50 Cal.App.4th 1878, 1886.) "In the absence of an allegation that [Plaintiff] had any current mental injury as a result of [Defendant's] conduct, her present mental condition was not directly relevant to her allegation that she had suffered emotional distress in the past." (Id.) Here, Plaintiff has disclaimed any intent to seek damages for emotional distress other than past emotional distress. (Opp., 9:4-6; Ex. A, 190-192) Thus, an order compelling an examination into her current mental state is not appropriate. Defendants argue that the degree of Plaintiff's emotional distress has put her mental condition in controversy. (Reply 2:10-12; 3:23-25.) Not so. When the defendant in Doyle sought to test the extent of the plaintiff's alleged distress, the court held the only statutorily authorized justification for ordering a mental examination is that the "mental condition" of the examinee is "in controversy." (Doyle, 50 Cal.App.4th at 1886.) And, here, again, Plaintiff states her current mental state is not in controversy. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 302 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 302 Zoom ID 160 409 7690; Passcode 516287.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the court's website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under "Online Services," navigate to "Tentative Rulings," and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: their name, CSR and telephone numbers, and their individual work email address. (302/JMQ)
